commander_summary: CONTESTED thesis: Municipal water distribution Sector 4 is in an active stress event with primary pump offline by designed cavitation protection while 24,000 GPM unidentified demand drains gravity reserves. Kill Switch #2 (contamination at 20 PSI) is at WARNING — Sector 4 pressure is 26.5 PSI with 6.5 PSI margin and all structural forces pushing downward. The core paradox: every automated protection mechanism is working exactly as designed, yet the same protective actions that validate the architecture are consuming the finite buffer that keeps the system viable. The call is INCREASE MONITORING — no kill switch is triggered and the system is operating within designed degraded mode, but the operator action window is hours, not days, and the single most consequential variable (how long the demand lasts) is completely unobservable. The next telemetry reading will establish whether this is manageable stress or an accelerating cascade.
score_reasoning: INCREASE_MONITORING is the correct action for a CONTESTED thesis with WARNING on Kill Switch #2. The system is operating within its designed degraded mode and no kill switch is triggered, which precludes EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. However, the 6.5 PSI margin on Kill Switch #2 with unknown rate of decline and no confirmed countervailing mechanism means the situation could deteriorate to CRITICAL within minutes to hours. Maximum telemetry refresh frequency should be established. The next Sector 4 pressure reading and reserve level reading are operationally critical — they will establish trends per LZ-RC-002 and convert snapshot uncertainty into actionable trajectory data.

chain: Primary mechanical supply fully offline — 0% pump contribution

Demand source identity and duration completely unknown — the system cannot predict or control the load
compounding_risks.outcome: A designed pump trip simultaneously VALIDATES the thesis architecture (equipment protection working exactly as specified) AND THREATENS thesis viability (removing the only confirmed active supply to Sector 4) because the protective mechanism that prevents Kill Switch #5 (destructive mechanical failure) directly enables Kill Switch #2 (contamination from pressure loss) and Kill Switch #4 (reserve depletion during outage). The system can perform flawlessly and still breach kill switches if the demand event outlasts the reserves.

This is not a contradiction between data and behavior — both are accurate descriptions of the same reality from different perspectives. The data shows the system working as designed. The behavioral analysis shows that the designed response has a time limit. Both are true. The paradox is genuine: the thesis describes resilience architecture that works perfectly for transient events but has an unstated time horizon. The system is performing exactly as specified while the safety margin narrows. Forcing resolution in either direction would be dishonest. Per LZ-EH-005, when perspectives of equal weight point in opposite directions, the tension itself is the finding.

The data (97.5% level) is accurate. The behavioral concern (unknown runway) is also valid. These are not contradictory — they are complementary observations about the same data point. The percentage is reassuring; the missing denominator prevents confidence. Holding the paradox is correct because resolving it in either direction requires assumptions about absolute capacity that do not exist in the data. Per LZ-RC-002, the single reading cannot establish a depletion trend.

bias_check.assessment: The thesis is contested because the evidence simultaneously validates and threatens it. Every automated protection mechanism is performing exactly as designed — the pump trip saved equipment (Kill Switch #5 GREEN), gravity reserves deployed at near-full capacity, grid segmentation is containing the stress (Kill Switch #1 GREEN), and operators are following protocol. This is the thesis working. Yet these same designed responses are consuming a finite buffer with no confirmed mechanism to halt the drain, and the single variable that determines whether the thesis holds (demand duration) is completely unobservable. The thesis is CONTESTED — not WEAKENING, because the architecture is demonstrably functional; not STABLE, because Kill Switch #2 is at WARNING with a narrowing margin. The outcome hinges on a variable outside the system's control and outside our observation.

kill_switches.name: Kill Switch #1: System-Wide Pressure Collapse (grid_average_pressure_psi below 25.0 PSI for consecutive readings)
kill_switches.detail: 42 PSI with a 25 PSI threshold provides 17 PSI of headroom. Even accounting for the localized Sector 4 stress, the grid average is demonstrating effective segmentation. No evidence or structural reasoning suggests systemic cascade is imminent. Single reading caveat applies, but the margin is substantial enough to classify GREEN rather than MONITORING.

kill_switches.name: Kill Switch #2: Critical Contamination Risk (sector_4_pressure_psi below 20.0 PSI)
kill_switches.detail: This is the most proximate kill switch threat. 26.5 PSI with a 20.0 PSI threshold leaves 6.5 PSI margin. Every structural condition (pump offline, extreme demand, surge valve open) drives pressure downward. Gravity reserves are the only countervailing force and their effective contribution to Sector 4 pressure is unquantified. The next telemetry reading is critical — any reading below approximately 23 PSI should trigger preparation for sector isolation. WARNING is maintained (not escalated to TRIGGERED) because 26.5 PSI is above 20.0 PSI and I have no data establishing the rate of decline. But operational readiness for isolation should be at maximum.

kill_switches.name: Kill Switch #3: Catastrophic Uncontained Leak (flow > 20,000 GPM AND reserves dropping >15%/min AND pumps active)
kill_switches.detail: Only one of three compound conditions is met. The compound design means this kill switch is structurally blocked in the current state. Elevated to MONITORING from mere tracking because the flow component is 20% above threshold and the pump restart scenario is a legitimate concern for any restart decision. However, the current risk profile does not warrant WARNING because 2 of 3 conditions are not met.

kill_switches.name: Kill Switch #4: Reserve Depletion During Outage (reserves below 10.0% while primary pumps offline)
kill_switches.detail: Reserves at 97.5% provide substantial headroom to the 10% threshold. However, the structural preconditions are established: pumps are offline and reserves are the sole supply under extreme demand. The depletion rate is unknown but the direction is unambiguously downward. MONITORING rather than WARNING because the current level is very healthy — but the inability to calculate runway prevents dismissing this kill switch. Each subsequent reading is critical to establish the depletion trend.

kill_switches.name: Kill Switch #5: Destructive Mechanical Failure (CRITICAL_DAMAGE or failure to trip during sustained WARNING_ELEVATED)
kill_switches.detail: This kill switch is clearly not triggered and the current evidence actively contradicts its conditions. The pump trip IS the designed prevention mechanism for Kill Switch #5. The sensor worked, the protection logic executed, and the pump was saved from cavitation damage. This is the most thesis-confirming data point for equipment protection.

Root cause analysis typically requires separate diagnostic procedures (physical inspection, intake pressure readings, vibration analysis) that are not captured in SCADA telemetry. This is a standard limitation of fail-safe sensor architecture.

Gravity reserves are the only countervailing force and their effective contribution to Sector 4 pressure is unquantified. The next telemetry reading is critical — any reading below approximately 23 PSI should trigger preparation for sector isolation. WARNING is maintained (not escalated to TRIGGERED) because 26.5 PSI is above 20.0 PSI and I have no data establishing the rate of decline. But operational readiness for isolation should be at maximum.

kill_switch_review.criterion: Kill Switch #3: Catastrophic Uncontained Leak (flow > 20,000 GPM AND reserves dropping >15%/min AND pumps active)
kill_switch_review.layer3_context: 1 of 3 conditions met. The pump trip structurally blocks conditions 2 and 3. Paradoxically, the event that creates the stress also prevents this kill switch from triggering. Pump restart under persistent demand could activate all three conditions — this is a valid prediction but not a current measurement.
kill_switch_review.reasoning: Only one of three compound conditions is met. The compound design means this kill switch is structurally blocked in the current state. Elevated to MONITORING from mere tracking because the flow component is 20% above threshold and the pump restart scenario is a legitimate concern for any restart decision. However, the current risk profile does not warrant WARNING because 2 of 3 conditions are not met.

kill_switch_review.criterion: Kill Switch #4: Reserve Depletion During Outage (reserves below 10.0% while primary pumps offline)
kill_switch_review.layer3_context: Both preconditions are structurally present (reserves being drawn down, pumps offline), but reserves are at 97.5% — 87.5 percentage points above the 10% threshold. Depletion rate is unknown per single data point. The self-reinforcing depletion loop is active but currently in the orderly phase.
kill_switch_review.reasoning: Reserves at 97.5% provide substantial headroom to the 10% threshold. However, the structural preconditions are established: pumps are offline and reserves are the sole supply under extreme demand. The depletion rate is unknown but the direction is unambiguously downward. MONITORING rather than WARNING because the current level is very healthy — but the inability to calculate runway prevents dismissing this kill switch. Each subsequent reading is critical to establish the depletion trend.

kill_switch_review.criterion: Kill Switch #5: Destructive Mechanical Failure (CRITICAL_DAMAGE or failure to trip during sustained WARNING_ELEVATED)
kill_switch_review.layer3_context: The pump tripped as designed — this is the OPPOSITE of Kill Switch #5. The cavitation sensor detected the threat and shut down the pump to prevent damage. The protection mechanism worked. Sensor state is TRIPPED_OFFLINE, not CRITICAL_DAMAGE.
kill_switch_review.reasoning: This kill switch is clearly not triggered and the current evidence actively contradicts its conditions. The pump trip IS the designed prevention mechanism for Kill Switch #5. The sensor worked, the protection logic executed, and the pump was saved from cavitation damage. This is the most thesis-confirming data point for equipment protection.

_layer4_raw.thesis_status_reasoning: The thesis is contested because the evidence simultaneously validates and threatens it. Every automated protection mechanism is performing exactly as designed — the pump trip saved equipment (Kill Switch #5 GREEN), gravity reserves deployed at near-full capacity, grid segmentation is containing the stress (Kill Switch #1 GREEN), and operators are following protocol. This is the thesis working. Yet these same designed responses are consuming a finite buffer with no confirmed mechanism to halt the drain, and the single variable that determines whether the thesis holds (demand duration) is completely unobservable. The thesis is CONTESTED — not WEAKENING, because the architecture is demonstrably functional; not STABLE, because Kill Switch #2 is at WARNING with a narrowing margin. The outcome hinges on a variable outside the system's control and outside our observation.
_layer4_raw.action_reasoning: INCREASE_MONITORING is the correct action for a CONTESTED thesis with WARNING on Kill Switch #2. The system is operating within its designed degraded mode and no kill switch is triggered, which precludes EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. However, the 6.5 PSI margin on Kill Switch #2 with unknown rate of decline and no confirmed countervailing mechanism means the situation could deteriorate to CRITICAL within minutes to hours. Maximum telemetry refresh frequency should be established. The next Sector 4 pressure reading and reserve level reading are operationally critical — they will establish trends per LZ-RC-002 and convert snapshot uncertainty into actionable trajectory data.

acquisition_dispositions.reasoning: Demand source identity and duration is the single most consequential unknown in the assessment (impact score 5). It determines whether the thesis is confirmed or falsified. Any data that identifies the demand source, its cause, or expected duration would collapse the assessment from contested to directional. This is the highest-value acquisition possible.

Absolute gravity reserve capacity converts 97.5% from a comfort number to an actionable runway calculation. Combined with sequential readings to establish depletion rate, this would resolve T-3 and partially inform T-1 (by establishing how long the system can sustain before kill switch). High value.

Secondary pump existence determines recovery path options. If confirmed, it provides an active recovery mechanism independent of demand behavior. If denied, it confirms the system is limited to gravity reserves and demand subsidence. Resolves T-6.

Surge relief valve flow rate and closure logic are important but secondary to demand identification, reserve capacity, and secondary pump existence. The valve's impact modulates the reserve depletion rate but is not independently decisive. If the next telemetry reading shows the valve has closed (resolving T-5), this acquisition becomes unnecessary. Defer to next cycle.

Prior Sector 4 pressure reading with timestamp would establish rate of decline — the most operationally urgent data point after demand identification. Two pressure readings would convert the snapshot to a trajectory, enabling time-to-kill-switch calculation. Directly resolves T-2.

monitoring_triggers: Sector 4 pressure below 23 PSI on next reading → escalate to REDUCE_LOAD and prepare sector isolation
Sector 4 pressure below 20 PSI → EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN — Kill Switch #2 triggered, thesis FALSIFIED
Gravity reserve level below 85% on next reading → escalate to REDUCE_LOAD (indicates rapid depletion)
Gravity reserve level below 10% with pumps offline → EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN — Kill Switch #4 triggered
Surge relief valve transitions to CLOSED → reduce T-5 impact score, remove parasitic drain concern
Demand flow drops below 15,000 GPM → significant positive signal, reassess pump restart feasibility
Pump cavitation sensor transitions from TRIPPED_OFFLINE to any other state → immediate assessment of restart viability
Grid average pressure drops below 35 PSI → escalate Kill Switch #1 to WARNING, stress may be spreading
Any consecutive grid average pressure reading below 25 PSI → EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN — Kill Switch #1 triggered
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: The identity, cause, and expected duration of the 24,000 GPM demand event. This single unknown determines whether the thesis is confirmed (transient event, reserves bridge successfully) or falsified (sustained event, reserves exhausted, Kill Switch #2 breached). Every other assessment — reserve adequacy, pressure trajectory, pump restart viability — is downstream of this variable.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: The next telemetry snapshot changes everything. If Sector 4 pressure is at 24 PSI or below, the assessment moves to CRITICAL with preparation for EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. If Sector 4 pressure is stable at 26-27 PSI, the assessment stabilizes and gravity reserves are demonstrably contributing. If demand flow drops significantly from 24,000 GPM, the assessment improves materially. If reserve level shows meaningful decline from 97.5%, the depletion clock becomes real and quantifiable. Any identification of the demand source (type, expected duration) would be the single most valuable data point.
_layer4_raw.final_report: CONTESTED thesis: Municipal water distribution Sector 4 is in an active stress event with primary pump offline by designed cavitation protection while 24,000 GPM unidentified demand drains gravity reserves. Kill Switch #2 (contamination at 20 PSI) is at WARNING — Sector 4 pressure is 26.5 PSI with 6.5 PSI margin and all structural forces pushing downward. The core paradox: every automated protection mechanism is working exactly as designed, yet the same protective actions that validate the architecture are consuming the finite buffer that keeps the system viable. The call is INCREASE MONITORING — no kill switch is triggered and the system is operating within designed degraded mode, but the operator action window is hours, not days, and the single most consequential variable (how long the demand lasts) is completely unobservable. The next telemetry reading will establish whether this is manageable stress or an accelerating cascade.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Trip → Pressure Decline → Reserve Depletion → Reduced Head Pressure → Declining Gravity Flow → Accelerated Pressure Decline (self-reinforcing negative loop)
components: Physics: gravity-fed flow decreases as tank level drops
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Transition to active_accelerating occurs when gravity reserve depletion reaches a level where head pressure differential can no longer maintain sufficient flow to Sector 4 to offset the 24,000 GPM demand. This is a physics-based inflection point, not a sudden failure — but I cannot calculate when it occurs because I lack absolute tank capacity, current flow rate from reserves, and the head pressure curve. The loop becomes self-reinforcing once gravity flow drops below net demand: lower tank → less head pressure → less flow → faster pressure decline → more flow needed.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is THE critical loop for the thesis. The thesis claims passive reserves can maintain baseline viability. This loop determines whether that claim holds. If the loop remains orderly (reserves drain slowly, demand subsides, or pump restarts), the thesis is confirmed. If the loop accelerates past the gravity system's ability to compensate, Sector 4 pressure will reach 20 PSI and Kill Switch #2 triggers. The loop is currently orderly because reserves are at 97.5%, but orderly-to-accelerating transition speed is unknown.

feedback_loops.loop: Surge Relief Valve Parasitic Drain → Reserve Depletion Acceleration → Reduced Effective Supply to Sector 4 (compounding negative loop)
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: If the surge relief valve remains open, it diverts an unknown portion of gravity reserve supply away from Sector 4 into the holding basin. This competes directly with the primary feedback loop above — every gallon diverted through the valve is a gallon not reaching Sector 4. Transition to active_accelerating depends on valve closure timing (unknown per SIG-011) and the valve's flow rate relative to total gravity supply (unknown).
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The surge relief valve creates a parasitic drain that the thesis does not account for as a sustained condition. The thesis treats it as transient protection. If it is persistent, it accelerates the primary depletion loop. This is a potential thesis modification point — the thesis may underestimate the cost of the water hammer protection mechanism during a sustained demand event.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Restart Paradox — Demand Persistence Prevents Safe Restart → Extended Outage → Reserve Depletion (trapped position)
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: If cavitation was demand-induced (the simpler explanation per LZ-RC-004), the pump cannot safely restart until demand drops below the pump's capacity threshold. But demand is driven by an external uncontrolled actor. This creates a trapped position: the system needs the pump online to maintain pressure, but cannot safely bring the pump online while the condition that tripped it persists. The only escape routes are: (1) demand naturally subsides, (2) a secondary pump exists and can be activated (unknown per SIG-010), or (3) operators manually override the cavitation protection (which would risk Kill Switch #5 — destructive mechanical failure). Option 3 contradicts the thesis's core principle of 'protect hardware first.'
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is a potential thesis paradox. The thesis says the system sacrifices primary components to protect the whole. But if the demand event persists long enough, the protective sacrifice (pump trip) becomes the mechanism that allows Kill Switch #2 or #4 to trigger. The thesis's resilience architecture works for transient demand events. Whether it works for sustained demand events is the untested question — and the demand duration is the single most consequential unknown.

feedback_loops.loop: Kill Switch #3 Dormant Re-activation — Pump Restart Under Persistent Demand Could Trigger Compound Kill Switch
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Currently dormant because the pump trip blocks conditions 2 and 3 of Kill Switch #3. However, if the pump is restarted while demand persists AND reserves have begun depleting significantly, all three conditions could activate simultaneously: (1) flow > 20,000 GPM — already met, (2) reserves dropping >15%/min — possible if tanks are partially depleted and demand is extreme, (3) pumps active — would become true on restart. This is a prediction per LZ-RC-005, not a measurement.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The pump trip paradoxically protects against Kill Switch #3 while exposing Kill Switch #2 and #4. Restarting the pump could shift the risk profile. This creates a decision dilemma for any restart attempt — the act of restoring primary supply could trigger the catastrophic leak detection kill switch if reserves are already in decline.

System architecture creates inherent trapped positions during sustained demand events — the protective response (pump trip) that prevents one failure mode (cavitation damage, Kill Switch #5) directly enables other failure modes (pressure decline toward Kill Switch #2, reserve depletion toward Kill Switch #4)
The demand source is the only actor with agency to change the trajectory of this event, and it is the only actor whose behavior is completely unobservable

strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The 24,000 GPM represents actual external demand (e.g., fire suppression, industrial draw) being served by the distribution grid. The demand source is an external actor doing what external actors do — consuming water at high volume for operational reasons unrelated to the grid's internal strategy.

strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The 24,000 GPM includes significant parasitic flow through the open surge relief valve, meaning actual end-user demand may be lower than the total system flow suggests. This would mean the system stress is partially self-inflicted by its own protective mechanism.

However, there is a valid secondary observation with only 1 assumption: the open surge relief valve may be inflating the total flow number. If the valve is diverting, say, 2,000-5,000 GPM to the holding basin, then actual end-user demand is 19,000-22,000 GPM — still extreme but potentially below the 20,000 GPM kill switch component. Kill Switch #1 is not threatened. The null hypothesis — standard network segmentation — is fully sufficient.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Pump cavitation root cause undiagnosed — demand-induced vs. supply-side failure
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The cavitation was demand-induced. The 24,000 GPM draw exceeded the pump's hydraulic capacity, creating low-pressure zones in the pump casing that triggered the acoustic sensor. This is the thesis's described mechanism and the simpler explanation given the simultaneous extreme demand measurement.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — null hypothesis (demand-induced cavitation) is the simpler explanation per LZ-RC-004 and holds as the working hypothesis.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Supply-side failure (intake blockage, main break, or mechanical issue) caused cavitation independent of demand levels. This would mean the pump cannot restart even if demand subsides.

Thesis context: 'cavitation occurs when demand outpaces supply water'

If supply-side: pump restart would re-trigger cavitation regardless of demand level, or diagnostic data would show intake/mechanical problems.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-RC-004, the simpler explanation is tested first. The simultaneous presence of 24,000 GPM demand and cavitation strongly suggests demand-induced causation. However, this cannot be confirmed without either diagnostic data or observing restart behavior. The alternative (supply-side failure) is less likely given the demand context but cannot be ruled out. This matters enormously for recovery: demand-induced = self-correcting scenario; supply-side = potentially extended outage requiring physical repair.

strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Secondary pump stations exist but are not included in the current telemetry feed, or exist for other sectors but not Sector 4, or exist and have not yet been activated by SCADA.

strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Activation of a secondary pump station in telemetry would disprove the null hypothesis. Absence of secondary activation over multiple readings increases probability that the null hypothesis is correct but can never confirm it.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The data architecture shows one pump station. The operator references one pump station. Per LZ-RC-004, the simpler explanation (one station serving Sector 4) should be tested first. I cannot confirm this but it is the working hypothesis. The critical implication: if there is only one pump station, the system's recovery options are limited to pump restart or demand subsidence. Gravity reserves are the only buffer, and their adequacy is unknown.

hidden_moves.incentive_basis: Industrial SCADA systems are typically designed with post-trip diagnostic and restart preparation logic. It would be rational for the system to be evaluating whether conditions are safe for restart.

hidden_moves.materialization_signal: Pump station load rising from 0% would confirm restart activity. Cavitation sensor transitioning from TRIPPED_OFFLINE to another state would confirm diagnostic progression.

hidden_moves.likely_action: Operators may be performing off-log activities including coordination with the demand source, preparation of isolation valves for Sector 4, or communication with regulatory authorities.

Per CL-002, CL-003, CL-005, CL-007, CL-008, CL-009, CL-012, CL-016, CL-020, and CL-022, this inference class has been identified and stripped 10+ times — the most thoroughly vetted and consistently rejected inference in the entire analysis chain.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: All automated systems are executing designed protocols correctly. SCADA tripped the pump as designed (Kill Switch #5 NOT triggered). Gravity reserves deployed at near-full capacity as designed. Grid segmentation is containing the stress (Kill Switch #1 NOT threatened). Operators are following stated protocol. The thesis's architecture IS working — every mechanism is performing as specified. The question is whether the demand event duration will allow the designed resilience to hold. If the demand event is transient (minutes to tens of minutes), reserves should sustain viability and the thesis is confirmed.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Minutes to 1-2 hours. Confirmation requires: demand subsiding, pressure stabilizing above 20 PSI, reserves remaining above 10%, pump restart capability confirmed.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis may need to be modified to acknowledge that the passive reserve system's adequacy is conditional on demand event duration in a way the thesis does not currently quantify. The thesis states the system 'maintains structural integrity under extreme asymmetric load through automated redundancy and gravity-fed reserve deployment' — but does not specify the time horizon or demand magnitude limits of this claim. Modification would add: 'The gravity reserve bridge is time-limited and demand-magnitude-dependent. For sustained demand events exceeding [X] duration at [Y] GPM, the reserves are insufficient and mechanical supply restoration is required.' Additionally, the surge relief valve's role as a parasitic drain during sustained outages may need explicit treatment.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: Reserves at 97.5% without known absolute capacity or depletion rate. Sector 4 pressure at 26.5 PSI with 6.5 PSI margin. Demand source duration unknown. Surge relief valve creating competing demand on reserves. No secondary pump confirmed. The thesis is not wrong — it is incomplete. It describes a resilient architecture that works for a specific (unstated) demand envelope.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Sector 4 pressure is 6.5 PSI from Kill Switch #2 with all structural forces pushing it downward and no confirmed mechanism to reverse the decline except demand reduction (externally controlled) or pump restart (currently blocked by the condition that caused the trip). If demand persists for long enough, the system will exhaust gravity reserves and Sector 4 pressure will breach 20 PSI. The question is purely temporal — how long does the demand last vs. how long can reserves sustain? Both are unknown.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Sector 4 pressure reading below 22-23 PSI on the next telemetry snapshot would indicate rapid deterioration and significantly increase falsification probability. Reserve level dropping below 90% would confirm meaningful depletion has begun. Any consecutive reading below 25 PSI on grid average would put Kill Switch #1 in play.

x402_paper_trades.question: What is the identity, cause, and expected duration of the 24,000 GPM demand event?
x402_paper_trades.data_source: Fire department dispatch/radio communications, industrial facility emergency reports, or the demand source itself. Alternatively, a second time-stamped telemetry reading showing demand trajectory (increasing, stable, or declining) would partially address this.
x402_paper_trades.impact_on_analysis: This is the single most consequential unknown. If the demand event is a 20-minute fire suppression operation, the thesis is almost certainly confirmed — reserves can bridge easily. If it is a 4-hour industrial emergency, the thesis is likely falsified — reserves almost certainly cannot sustain 24,000 GPM for that duration. Demand duration determines whether every other assessment (reserve adequacy, pressure trajectory, pump restart urgency) is critical or manageable.
x402_paper_trades.estimated_value: This data point alone could shift the assessment from 'uncertain with 25% falsification risk' to 'high confidence confirmation or falsification.' It is worth more than all other gaps combined.

x402_paper_trades.question: What is the absolute volumetric capacity of the gravity reserves in gallons, and what is the current effective gravity-fed flow rate to Sector 4?
x402_paper_trades.data_source: Water system engineering documentation, SCADA configuration database, or flow meters on the gravity feed lines.
x402_paper_trades.impact_on_analysis: Converts the 97.5% reading from a comfort signal to an actionable runway calculation. If the tanks hold 500,000 gallons, 97.5% = 487,500 gallons. At a hypothetical 5,000 GPM gravity flow (rough estimate for illustration only — actual rate unknown), that is approximately 97 minutes of supply. If the tanks hold 2,000,000 gallons, the runway extends proportionally. This data, combined with demand duration, would make reserve adequacy scoreable.
x402_paper_trades.estimated_value: High — converts a percentage to an absolute timeline. Combined with demand duration data, this would resolve the coupled unknown that is the primary unscored assessment.

x402_paper_trades.question: Does the Sector 4 grid architecture include secondary or backup pump stations? If so, what is their current status?
x402_paper_trades.data_source: Grid architecture documentation, SCADA system configuration, or water utility engineering records.
x402_paper_trades.impact_on_analysis: Determines whether the system has ANY mechanical recovery path beyond restarting the tripped pump. If secondary pumps exist and are available, the system has a viable active recovery option independent of demand behavior. If no secondary pumps exist, gravity reserves are the sole buffer and the system is entirely dependent on either demand subsidence or primary pump restart — both externally contingent. This could change the thesis modification probability significantly.
x402_paper_trades.estimated_value: Moderate to high — fundamentally changes the recovery path assessment.

x402_paper_trades.question: What is the surge relief valve's flow rate and closure trigger logic?
x402_paper_trades.data_source: Valve specification documentation, SCADA control logic configuration, or a flow meter on the surge relief line.
x402_paper_trades.impact_on_analysis: Quantifies the parasitic drain on reserves and determines whether the drain is temporary (auto-closes) or persistent (requires manual intervention or specific conditions). If the valve is passing 3,000+ GPM and stays open indefinitely, it meaningfully accelerates reserve depletion. If it passes 500 GPM and auto-closes in 2 minutes, it is irrelevant.
x402_paper_trades.estimated_value: Moderate — modulates the reserve depletion calculation but is secondary to demand duration and absolute reserve capacity.

x402_paper_trades.question: What was the Sector 4 pressure reading at the previous telemetry snapshot, and what was the timestamp?
x402_paper_trades.data_source: SCADA telemetry archive — previous reading for sector_4_pressure_psi.
x402_paper_trades.impact_on_analysis: A second pressure reading with timestamp would establish a rate of decline per LZ-RC-002 (two points = trend). This converts 'pressure is low' to 'pressure is declining at X PSI per minute' — enabling calculation of estimated time to Kill Switch #2 (20 PSI). This is the most operationally urgent data point after demand identification.
x402_paper_trades.estimated_value: High — converts a snapshot to a trajectory. Critical for determining urgency of response.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly. All automated systems are executing designed protocols. The stress cascade is proceeding through predicted pathways: pump trip → gravity reserve activation → localized pressure decline. There are no unexpected failures, no cascading system errors, and no deviation from designed behavior. However, the margin between 'orderly stress' and 'disorderly cascade' is narrow and time-dependent. The orderly state is maintained by gravity reserves, which are a depleting resource. The transition from orderly to disorderly is not sudden — it is the gradual exhaustion of the buffer that keeps the system in designed degraded mode.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The most proximate catalyst for transition to 'approaching_critical' is Sector 4 pressure dropping below approximately 22-23 PSI — at that point, the margin to Kill Switch #2 is so thin that any additional stress (demand spike, valve malfunction, reserve flow degradation) could push through. The catalyst is not a discrete event but a threshold crossing. The second catalyst is reserve depletion rate becoming observable — once reserves drop visibly from 97.5% on sequential readings, the clock becomes real and quantifiable. Currently, the catalyst proximity is UNKNOWN because the rate of decline is unknown. It could be minutes away or hours away.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: The system is pre-loaded on three legs simultaneously: (1) Primary mechanical supply is fully offline — the active leg of the supply architecture contributes zero. (2) Demand is extreme and externally controlled — the system cannot modulate the load. (3) The surge relief valve is open as a parasitic drain — the system's own protective mechanism is competing for the limited passive supply. Each of these is individually manageable. Their simultaneous presence is the compound stress. The pre-load is structural, not trending — it was created by a single event (the demand surge causing cavitation causing pump trip causing surge valve opening) and all legs activated within the same event window.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis faces a genuine paradox: the system's designed resilience architecture is simultaneously its vulnerability under sustained load. The pump trip PROTECTS the hardware (thesis-confirming) but REMOVES active supply (thesis-stressing). The surge valve PROTECTS the mains (thesis-confirming) but DRAINS reserves (thesis-stressing). The gravity reserves SUSTAIN baseline supply (thesis-confirming) but ARE FINITE and declining under load (thesis-stressing). Every protective mechanism that fires correctly moves the system one step closer to a kill switch trigger — not because the system is failing, but because it is succeeding at protection while the external stress persists. The thesis is not wrong — the architecture IS resilient. But resilience is not the same as infinite endurance. The thesis describes an architecture that can absorb shock. Whether it can absorb sustained pressure depends on variables (demand duration, reserve capacity) that the thesis does not bound. The highest-risk scenario for thesis falsification (sustained extreme demand) is also the scenario that most validates the thesis's design principles (every protection mechanism fires correctly). The system could perform flawlessly and still breach Kill Switch #2 if the demand outlasts the reserves. This is an honest paradox, not a failure of analysis.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals that all observable actors — SCADA and grid operators — are executing their designed protocols without deviation. There is no say-do gap for any observable player. The strategic picture is dominated not by what players are doing (they are doing exactly what they should), but by what is UNKNOWN: the demand source's identity, magnitude trajectory, and expected duration. This single unknown is load-bearing for the entire assessment. The system's compound stress is building through four simultaneously active feedback loops, all of which converge on Kill Switch #2 (Sector 4 contamination at 20 PSI) as the most proximate threat. The thesis's core paradox — that protective mechanisms that work correctly under sustained load gradually consume the buffer that keeps the system viable — is the central dynamic Layer 4 should reconcile. The assessment cannot be resolved to high confidence without demand source identification; absent that data, the thesis exists in a superposition of 'confirmed if transient' and 'falsified if sustained' with no current evidence to collapse the probability.

knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Thesis documents that SCADA automated logic is 'programmed to protect the physical infrastructure first' and will 'ruthlessly shut down expensive pumps to save them from cavitation.' Cavitation acoustic sensor trip is the designed fail-safe behavior. The telemetry confirms pump load at 0%, sensor state TRIPPED_OFFLINE, and operator log confirms. This is a designed protection event, not an uncontrolled failure.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: NONE — the thesis explicitly describes this scenario and the system's designed response.
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Would have scored as purely negative (critical infrastructure offline)
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as designed fail-safe activation with known consequence (loss of primary mechanical supply to Sector 4). The trip itself is thesis-confirming; the consequence is thesis-stressing.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Shifted from pure negative to a CONTRADICTORY signal — the protection mechanism worked as designed, but the consequence creates pressure on other thesis components.

knowledge_audit.signal: Gravity reserve deployment initiated at near-full capacity
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Thesis identifies gravity-fed reserves as the core passive redundancy mechanism. Reserves at 97.5% indicates near-full capacity at activation time. Operator log confirms initiation. This is the thesis's primary backup engaging as designed.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: I do not know the volumetric capacity of the gravity reserves in absolute terms (gallons). 97.5% of an unknown total makes time-to-depletion impossible to calculate against a 24,000 GPM draw. I also do not know the actual flow rate the gravity system can deliver — it depends on head pressure differential, pipe sizing, and friction losses, none of which are in the data.
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Would have scored as strongly positive — reserves nearly full, backup working
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as positive but with critical uncertainty: 97.5% is a percentage without a denominator. Cannot assess runway without absolute capacity and gravity-fed flow rate data.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Reduced confidence from HIGH to MEDIUM due to inability to assess whether reserves can sustain 24,000 GPM demand for meaningful duration.

knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Kill Switch #2 is sector_4_pressure_psi below 20.0 PSI. Current reading is 26.5 PSI. The 6.5 PSI margin is factual. The thesis states operators should 'prepare to isolate the sector only if pressure approaches 20 PSI.' At 26.5, we are 32.5% above the kill switch. However, with primary pumps offline and 24,000 GPM demand, the direction of pressure movement is downward and the rate of decline is unknown.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: I do not know the rate of pressure decline. This is a single snapshot. Without a prior reading and timestamp, I cannot calculate how fast pressure is dropping or how long until 20 PSI is reached.
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Would have scored as very high severity based on proximity to kill switch
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as high severity — 26.5 PSI with downward pressure and no pump support is genuinely dangerous, but the single data point prevents modeling time to kill switch. Severity remains high on the structural facts alone.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Confirmed high severity but added explicit uncertainty about the rate of deterioration. Per LZ-RC-002, single data point cannot establish a trend (rate of decline).

knowledge_audit.signal: Anomalous total flow exceeds catastrophic leak threshold baseline
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Kill Switch #3 requires flow > 20,000 GPM AND reserve dropping >15%/min AND pumps active. Currently: flow is 24,000 (condition 1 MET), reserves at 97.5% with no rapid drop evident (condition 2 NOT MET), pumps offline (condition 3 NOT MET). The kill switch is structurally not triggered because 2 of 3 conditions fail. However, the flow volume is 20% above the threshold component.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: I do not know whether the 24,000 GPM represents actual end-user demand or includes parasitic flows (surge relief valve diversion). The system_total_flow_gpm may overstate actual consumption if the surge relief valve is diverting a significant portion.
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Would have scored as critical severity based on raw number exceeding threshold
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as high severity but not kill-switch-triggering. The 24,000 GPM is alarming but the kill switch requires compound conditions, only one of which is met. The flow number may also be inflated by surge relief diversion.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Reduced from critical to high — the compound kill switch design means a single exceeded component is concerning but not triggering.

knowledge_audit.signal: Surge relief valve open — water hammer mitigation active
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Thesis defines surge relief valve as 'a mechanical safety bypass designed to dump water out of the high-pressure mains into a holding basin to prevent shockwaves (water hammer) if a pump suddenly stops.' The pump just stopped. This is expected behavior. However, I need to understand: the valve diverts flow away from end-users, meaning effective delivery capacity to Sector 4 is reduced by whatever volume the valve is passing.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: I do not know the flow rate through the surge relief valve. Without this, I cannot determine how much of the 24,000 GPM system flow is being diverted vs. reaching Sector 4. I also do not know the automated closure logic — when does this valve close? Is it pressure-triggered, timer-based, or manually closed?
knowledge_audit.pre_audit_assessment: Would have scored as moderate positive (protection working)
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as contradictory: positive that infrastructure is protected from water hammer, negative that it represents an unknown parasitic drain on the system competing with demand for limited supply.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Added the parasitic drain dimension. The signal is genuinely dual-natured — protection and penalty simultaneously.

knowledge_audit.signal: Grid average pressure holds well above collapse threshold
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Kill Switch #1 requires grid_average_pressure_psi below 25.0 PSI for consecutive readings. Current reading is 42 PSI, which is 17 PSI above the threshold — 68% headroom. This is the strongest thesis-supporting data point in the current snapshot. It confirms the pressure problem is localized to Sector 4, not systemic.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: NONE — the data is direct, the threshold is clearly defined, and the margin is substantial.
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Moderate positive — confirmed. Network segmentation is functionally containing the Sector 4 stress. However, this is a single reading (per LZ-RC-002) and consecutive readings are required for the kill switch, so the current healthy reading is reassuring but one snapshot.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Minimal change. Confirmed initial assessment with appropriate single-reading caveat.

knowledge_audit.signal: Operator protocol adherence — monitoring without manual override

The operator log shows exactly this behavior: confirming pump offline, noting surge relief, initiating gravity failover. Per LZ-EH-003, actions carry more weight than statements, and the log shows actions consistent with stated protocol.
knowledge_audit.gap_identified: The operator log is sparse — three short entries. I do not know if the log is comprehensive or if operators are performing additional actions not logged. Per corrections ledger (CL-009, CL-022 and 7+ entries), I MUST NOT infer off-log actions.
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Moderate positive based on visible evidence only. Operator actions in the log align with stated protocol. No inference about off-log activity.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Corrections ledger forced strict discipline: score only what is visible in the log. No credit or debit for hypothesized off-log actions.

knowledge_audit.signal: Kill Switch #3 partial trigger — flow threshold exceeded
knowledge_audit.knowledge_check: Verified: Kill Switch #3 is compound — requires ALL THREE conditions (flow > 20,000 GPM, reserves dropping >15%/min, pumps active). The kill switch is structurally blocked by the pump trip itself — an interesting negative feedback where the fail-safe that created the stress also prevents the kill switch from triggering.

knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Scored as high severity for tracking purposes. One of three conditions is met. The compound design means the kill switch is not close to triggering, but the partial activation warrants monitoring. The pump restart scenario is a valid concern to flag without scoring as imminent.
knowledge_audit.audit_impact: LZ-RC-005 differentiation between prediction and measurement prevented over-scoring the pump restart scenario.

scored_signals.reasoning: Primary mechanical water supply for Sector 4 is confirmed offline by three independent data points (cavitation sensor, load at 0%, operator log) — per LZ-EH-001, multi-source verification makes this highly reliable. The trip is the designed fail-safe behavior per thesis context, which is thesis-confirming for equipment protection but thesis-stressing for supply continuity. The system has no confirmed alternative mechanical supply (secondary pumps unconfirmed), making the grid entirely dependent on passive gravity reserves. Severity is high because this is the single event that removes the primary active supply mechanism, directly stressing Kill Switch #2 (Sector 4 pressure) and Kill Switch #4 (reserve depletion during outage).

scored_signals.signal: Gravity reserve deployment initiated at near-full capacity
scored_signals.reasoning: The thesis's core redundancy mechanism is activating with 97.5% capacity — this is the designed response and represents the system working as intended. Scored as 6 (positive-moderate) rather than higher because: (1) I cannot determine whether 97.5% of unknown absolute capacity provides sufficient runway at 24,000 GPM demand (THESIS_CAPABILITY_GAP), (2) gravity-fed flow rate diminishes as tanks drain (physics), (3) the surge relief valve is simultaneously drawing from the same reserves, creating competing demand on a single supply source. The 97.5% reading is reassuring as a snapshot but cannot be extrapolated to adequacy without absolute capacity data. Per LZ-RC-002, this single reading does not establish a depletion trend. Confidence is MEDIUM due to the capability gap.

At 26.5 PSI, Sector 4 is 6.5 PSI above the 20 PSI Kill Switch #2 (critical contamination risk). With primary pumps offline and 24,000 GPM demand, the structural conditions driving pressure downward are all active and none driving it upward are confirmed (gravity reserves are deploying but their effective flow rate to Sector 4 is unknown, and the surge relief valve is diverting some flow). The margin is factually thin. This is a DIRECT threat to the most immediately dangerous kill switch — contamination risk at 20 PSI. The rate of decline is unknown (single data point per LZ-RC-002), preventing timeline estimation, but the direction of pressure movement (downward) is established by the structural conditions (no pump, high demand, open surge valve).

scored_signals.signal: Anomalous total flow exceeds catastrophic leak threshold baseline
scored_signals.reasoning: 24,000 GPM exceeds the 20,000 GPM component of Kill Switch #3 by 20%. However, Kill Switch #3 is a compound trigger requiring three simultaneous conditions, only one of which is met. The flow volume signals extreme demand stress on the grid but does not indicate catastrophic uncontained leak because the reserve depletion pattern does not match (97.5% with no rapid drop) and pumps are offline. The high flow may partially reflect surge relief diversion rather than pure end-user demand. Severity is 7 because the volume is genuinely anomalous and stresses the entire system, but the compound kill switch design means this single-condition exceedance is not immediately system-threatening.

scored_signals.signal: Surge relief valve open — water hammer mitigation active
scored_signals.reasoning: The valve opening is the designed protective response to sudden pump shutdown — thesis-confirming for infrastructure protection. It prevents potentially catastrophic water hammer in the mains. However, it creates a parasitic drain of unknown magnitude competing with Sector 4 demand for gravity reserve supply. Without knowing the valve's flow rate or closure logic, the net impact on system viability is uncertain. Scored as 6 reflecting the contradictory nature: protection achieved at the cost of reduced effective supply capacity. Confidence is MEDIUM because the valve's flow rate and closure timing are unknown (THESIS_CAPABILITY_GAP).

scored_signals.signal: Grid average pressure holds well above collapse threshold
scored_signals.reasoning: 42 PSI grid average is 17 PSI (68%) above the 25 PSI Kill Switch #1 threshold. This is the strongest thesis-supporting signal in the current dataset. It confirms: (1) the pressure problem is localized to Sector 4, not systemic, (2) network segmentation is functioning to contain the stress event, (3) Kill Switch #1 is not under immediate threat. Severity scored low (3) because this signal REDUCES overall concern. However, this is a single reading and the kill switch requires consecutive readings below 25 PSI, so one healthy snapshot does not guarantee continued health.

scored_signals.signal: Operator protocol adherence — monitoring without manual override
scored_signals.reasoning: Operator log shows actions consistent with stated protocol: acknowledging automated events, initiating gravity failover, not overriding automated systems. Per LZ-EH-003, the logged actions carry evidentiary weight. However, confidence is MEDIUM because the log is sparse (three entries) and may not capture all operator activities. Per corrections ledger (CL-009, CL-022, and 7+ additional entries), I explicitly do not infer any off-log actions. The score reflects only what is visible: compliance with stated protocol during an acute stress event. Severity is low (3) because this is a positive signal — operators are not interfering with designed automation.

scored_signals.signal: Unknown demand source magnitude and duration
scored_signals.reasoning: This is the single most consequential unknown in the assessment. The entire trajectory of the stress event — whether reserves are adequate, whether pressure stabilizes or continues falling, whether pump restart is possible — depends on demand duration and magnitude trajectory, both of which are completely unknown. The 24,000 GPM current measurement is factual (Tier 1 telemetry), but the source identity, expected duration, and trajectory are structurally opaque. Per CL-017, any inference about the demand source's behavior requires 3+ assumptions about an unidentified actor and is SPECULATIVE. Severity is 9 because the unknown is directly load-bearing for multiple kill switches simultaneously, and the system has no mechanism to control or predict the demand source.

scored_signals.signal: Gravity reserve depletion rate under sustained 24,000 GPM demand
scored_signals.reasoning: The depletion rate is the bridge between 'reserves look full now' and 'reserves will last long enough.' Per LZ-RC-002, the single reading at 97.5% does not establish a trend. Per CL-023, demand source trajectory modeling has been explicitly rejected as speculative. I genuinely cannot score the adequacy of reserves because I lack: (1) a second time-stamped reading to calculate rate, (2) absolute tank capacity, (3) gravity-fed effective flow rate under current conditions. The physics (decreasing flow as tanks drain) adds a nonlinear dimension that makes simple linear extrapolation unreliable even if I had two data points. Severity is 7 reflecting the importance of this gap rather than a measured threat level. Confidence is LOW because this is scored with known, critical knowledge gaps. Kill Switch #4 (reserves below 10% with pumps offline) depends entirely on this unknown.

scored_signals.signal: No secondary pump station activation reported
scored_signals.reasoning: The absence of any data about secondary pump stations is ambiguous per LZ-RC-003 — it is not evidence that they don't exist, nor evidence that they do. However, the thesis mentions 'pump stations' (plural) while telemetry shows only one. Per CL-004, I cannot infer SCADA is managing secondary pumps invisibly. The practical impact: if the grid has only one pump station serving Sector 4, recovery from this event depends entirely on either (1) demand subsiding enough to restart Pump Station 1, or (2) gravity reserves outlasting the demand event. If secondary pumps exist but aren't activating, that could indicate a separate problem. Confidence is LOW because the grid architecture for mechanical redundancy is genuinely unknown to me.

scored_signals.signal: Surge relief valve closure timing unknown
scored_signals.reasoning: The valve should be transient protection, but closure logic is undocumented. If it closes soon, the parasitic drain is brief and irrelevant. If it remains open, it becomes a sustained competitor for gravity reserve supply, accelerating depletion and reducing effective delivery to Sector 4. The severity depends on duration of the open state, which is unknown. Scored at 5 as a moderate concern that could escalate (persistent open) or become irrelevant (rapid closure). This is INDIRECT to thesis viability because it modulates reserve depletion rate rather than directly threatening a kill switch.

Root cause determines the recovery path. Per LZ-RC-004, the simpler explanation (demand-induced cavitation from 24,000 GPM draw) should be tested first. However, supply-side failure (intake blockage, main break) remains a live possibility that cannot be ruled out without diagnostic data. If demand-induced, the pump may restart once demand subsides — this is a self-correcting scenario. If supply-side, physical repair may be required, extending the outage indefinitely. Per CL-001, I cannot infer SCADA is running diagnostics. The root cause is genuinely unknown. This is DIRECT to thesis viability because it determines whether mechanical supply can be restored (Kill Switch #5 relates to destructive mechanical failure, which is not indicated — the sensor properly tripped offline, which is the designed behavior, not a CRITICAL_DAMAGE state).

The 20 PSI state minimum is documented in the thesis. However, specific pre-threshold notification requirements (e.g., 25 PSI) are inferred from general regulatory practice, not from this system's documented requirements (THRESHOLD_CALIBRATION_GAP). The real concern is the 20 PSI kill switch, not the notification threshold. Regulatory notification is a secondary consequence of approaching kill switch territory, not an independent threat. Scored as Tier 2 (domain inference rather than core thesis telemetry) with 0.7x weighting. CONTEXTUAL because regulatory notification does not itself threaten system viability — it is a downstream administrative consequence.

scored_signals.signal: Pressure differential between grid average and Sector 4
scored_signals.reasoning: The 15.5 PSI differential is mathematically factual (42 - 26.5). The hydraulic gradient driving flow toward Sector 4 is physics. However, whether this creates a contamination risk from reverse flows depends on the inter-sector valve architecture, which is unknown (THESIS_CAPABILITY_GAP). The positive interpretation is that adjacent sectors may be partially supplementing Sector 4 supply through natural hydraulic gradient, slowing pressure decline. The negative interpretation is that non-standard water from dead-end mains could enter the active distribution zone. Both are plausible; neither can be confirmed without grid topology data. INDIRECT because the primary contamination risk is the 20 PSI groundwater infiltration threshold, not inter-sector flow.

scored_signals.signal: Kill Switch #3 partial trigger — flow threshold exceeded
scored_signals.reasoning: One of three compound conditions is met (flow > 20,000 GPM). The other two are NOT met (reserves not dropping >15%/min, pumps not active). The compound design of Kill Switch #3 means partial activation is a monitoring concern, not an imminent trigger. The observation that pump restart while demand persists could activate all three conditions simultaneously is a valid forward-looking risk per LZ-RC-005 (prediction, not measurement). Scored at 6: concerning enough to track closely but not indicating imminent kill switch activation. An interesting structural note: the pump trip that created the stress event also prevents Kill Switch #3 from fully triggering, as it requires pumps active.

unscored_signals.signal: Demand source identity/duration AND reserve depletion rate — the coupled unknown
unscored_signals.reason: These two unknowns are coupled: whether reserves are adequate depends on both depletion rate AND demand duration, neither of which can be determined from current data. Scoring reserve adequacy requires knowing demand trajectory; scoring demand risk requires knowing reserve capacity. Both are scored individually above for their current-state threat level, but the critical forward-looking question — 'will reserves outlast the demand event?' — cannot be answered and is flagged here as the primary unscored assessment.

compound_stress.pre_loaded_detail: The compound stress matrix is designed for financial market monitoring and does not apply to this municipal water infrastructure thesis. The analogous 'compound stress' in this context is the simultaneous convergence of: (1) primary pump offline, (2) extreme demand (24,000 GPM), (3) surge relief valve open (parasitic drain), (4) unknown demand source duration. These four stressors are compounding — each individually manageable, together they create a narrowing safety envelope around Kill Switch #2.
compound_stress.stress_chain_proximity: The system is in an active stress cascade. The pump trip removed active supply, extreme demand is drawing down reserves, and the surge valve is diverting a portion of remaining supply. The chain leads to Kill Switch #2 (Sector 4 pressure below 20 PSI) as the most proximate threat, with Kill Switch #4 (reserve depletion below 10% during outage) as the secondary threat if the event persists.

_layer2_raw.layer2_summary: ACTIVE STRESS EVENT: Primary pump tripped offline by designed cavitation protection while system experiences 24,000 GPM anomalous demand from an unidentified source. Gravity reserves deployed at 97.5% — the thesis's core redundancy mechanism is engaged. Kill Switch #2 (contamination at 20 PSI) is the most proximate threat with Sector 4 at 26.5 PSI and declining under structural conditions that all push pressure downward. Kill Switch #1 (grid collapse) is NOT threatened — grid average holds at 42 PSI, confirming localized stress. The three critical unknowns that Layer 3 must prioritize: (1) demand source identity and expected duration — this is the single most consequential unknown and determines whether reserves are adequate, (2) gravity reserve absolute capacity and depletion rate — only one snapshot at 97.5% exists, no trend can be established, (3) secondary pump station existence — it is unknown whether any mechanical redundancy exists beyond the tripped Pump Station 1. No corrections ledger entries were violated. All speculative inference classes (SCADA hidden diagnostics, operator off-log actions, demand source trajectory modeling) were identified and stripped per the established 23-entry corrections chain. The system is operating within its designed degraded-mode parameters, but the margin between 'designed resilience' and 'kill switch trigger' is narrowing under sustained unknown demand.

action_reasoning: INCREASE_MONITORING is the correct action for a CONTESTED thesis with WARNING on Kill Switch #2. The system is operating within its designed degraded mode and no kill switch is triggered, which precludes EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN. However, the 6.5 PSI margin on Kill Switch #2 with unknown rate of decline and no confirmed countervailing mechanism means the situation could deteriorate to CRITICAL within minutes to hours. Maximum telemetry refresh frequency should be established. The next Sector 4 pressure reading and reserve level reading are operationally critical — they will establish trends per LZ-RC-002 and convert snapshot uncertainty into actionable trajectory data.

_layer1_raw.signal: Gravity reserve deployment initiated at near-full capacity
_layer1_raw.description: High-zone gravity reserves are at 97.5%, and operator log confirms gravity failover initiation. The passive backup system is engaging with nearly full capacity, providing substantial runway for sustained demand. This is the thesis's core redundancy mechanism activating as designed.

Sector 4 pressure at 26.5 PSI is only 6.5 PSI above the 20 PSI critical contamination risk kill switch and only 1.5 PSI above the 25 PSI grid-average collapse threshold (applied locally). With primary pumps offline and demand at 24,000 GPM, the margin is thin. Any further pressure drop narrows the safety envelope rapidly.

However, the flow volume alone signals extreme demand — likely multi-hydrant fire suppression operations pulling massive volume from the grid.

_layer1_raw.signal: Surge relief valve open — water hammer mitigation active
_layer1_raw.description: The surge relief valve is OPEN, actively dumping high-pressure main water into the holding basin. This prevents destructive water hammer from the sudden pump shutdown. However, this diverts flow volume away from end-users, meaning effective delivery to Sector 4 is less than the 24,000 GPM system flow suggests. The open valve represents both successful protection and a parasitic flow loss.
_layer1_raw.evidence: surge_relief_valve_status: OPEN; pump just tripped offline creating transient conditions

_layer1_raw.description: Grid average pressure at 42 PSI remains 17 PSI above the 25 PSI system-wide collapse kill switch. Other sectors appear to be maintaining normal operating ranges, suggesting network segmentation is functioning.

_layer1_raw.description: Operator log shows procedural compliance: confirming pump offline status, noting surge relief activation, and initiating gravity failover.
